coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of biomass-derived N-doped carbon nanodots via a one-pot hydrothermal approach for enhanced lithium extraction in flow-electrode capacitive deionization
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Li
Zhao, Cuijiao
Wang, Shuyu
Zhao, Mengyu
Liu, Menglong
Zhou, Hongjian
Dai, Weiji
Zhang, Yudong
Cui, Can
Huang, Saifang
coredata.dc:description: Lithium, recognized as a limited natural resource, necessitates careful stewardship. The extraction and application of lithium resources have garnered significant interest within the research community. To facilitate economically viable and large-scale lithium extraction, there is an imperative demand for the development of efficient and cost-effective methodologies for lithium recovery. In this work, we propose an innovative strategy using nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNs) as a flow-electrode for electrochemical lithium extraction. N-CNs, synthesized from nitrogen-rich waste bean shells using a one-step hydrothermal method, are zero-dimensional structures with excellent fluidity, hydrophilicity, conductivity, and low Li+ ions diffusion resistance, which enhance their lithium extraction performance compared to activated carbon (AC). Flow-electrode capacitive deionization (FCDI) tests results demonstrate that the N-CNs flow-electrode exhibits a high average salt removal rate (ASRR) of 0.62 µmol min−1 cm−2 in 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution, a gravimetric electroadsorption capacity (GAC) of 75 mg g−1, and a charge efficiency (CE) of 97 %. Remarkably, the N-CNs flow-electrode demonstrates commendable stability and continuity throughout a continuous lithium extraction process lasting 30 h. These results confirm the potential application capability of N-CNs flow-electrode in industrial-scale lithium extraction, where they can serve as both electrode materials or conductive additive, further enhancing the overall performance of the FCDI device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped
Biomass-derived carbon nanodots
Lithium extraction
Flow-electrode capacitive deionization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CNs derived from bean shells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52202093), Jiangsu Specially Appointed Professorship Foundation (Grant No. 1064902003), and Jiangsu Provincial Double-Innovation Doctor Program (Grant No. JSSCBS20221297 and JSSCBS20221258).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process for N-CNs electrode slurry; (b)TEM image of N-CNs solution; (c) XRD pattern of N-CNs; (d) FTIR spectra of N-CNs.
XPS spectra of the N-CNs: (a) XPS total spectrum, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s.
(a) GCD curves of AC and N-CNs at 1 A g−1 in a three-electrode system; (b) EIS curves of AC and N-CNs in a two-electrode system.
(a) Time-dependent conductivity change and (b) ASRR for different electrode slurry in 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution at 2.5 V.

(a) Schematic diagram of operation in FCDI; (b) The conductivity varies with time; (c) Time-dependent GAC change; (d) Ragone curves; (e) The current changes with time and (f) ASRR and CE of FCDI devices by using N-CNs flow-electrode in 0.1 mol L−1 of LiCl solution at different voltages.

(a) Time-dependent current variation for N-CNs at different LiCl concentrations; (b) CE and ASRR of FCDI device by using N-CNs flow-electrode in different LiCl concentrations; (c) Time-dependent current variation and (d) N-CNs as flow-electrodes in FCDI for long-term cycling corresponding to ASRR and CE values.
Schematic diagram for EIS testing device.
(a-b) FCDI device structure and operation mode.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of biomass-derived N-doped carbon nanodots via a one-pot hydrothermal approach for enhanced lithium extraction in flow-electrode capacitive deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology, Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology, Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology, Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium, recognized as a limited natural resource, necessitates careful stewardship. The extraction and application of lithium resources have garnered significant interest within the research community. To facilitate economically viable and large-scale lithium extraction, there is an imperative demand for the development of efficient and cost-effective methodologies for lithium recovery. In this work, we propose an innovative strategy using nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNs) as a flow-electrode for electrochemical lithium extraction. N-CNs, synthesized from nitrogen-rich waste bean shells using a one-step hydrothermal method, are zero-dimensional structures with excellent fluidity, hydrophilicity, conductivity, and low Li+ ions diffusion resistance, which enhance their lithium extraction performance compared to activated carbon (AC). Flow-electrode capacitive deionization (FCDI) tests results demonstrate that the N-CNs flow-electrode exhibits a high average salt removal rate (ASRR) of 0.62 µmol min−1 cm−2 in 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution, a gravimetric electroadsorption capacity (GAC) of 75 mg g−1, and a charge efficiency (CE) of 97 %. Remarkably, the N-CNs flow-electrode demonstrates commendable stability and continuity throughout a continuous lithium extraction process lasting 30 h. These results confirm the potential application capability of N-CNs flow-electrode in industrial-scale lithium extraction, where they can serve as both electrode materials or conductive additive, further enhancing the overall performance of the FCDI device.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNs) with enhanced fluidity, hydrophilicity, conductivity, and reduced diffusion resistance for Li+ ions were synthesized from nitrogen-rich waste bean shells using a one-step hydrothermal method. These N-CNs solution directly serve as an effective flow-electrode for the electrochemical extraction of lithium.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In light of the swift proliferation of lithium-ion batteries within the new energy sector, lithium resources have assumed a critical position in the global electronic core industries. Consequently, their production has garnered significant attention and interest from numerous nations [1–3]. Currently, the extraction of lithium from natural resources, including salt brines and seawater, alongside the recycling of lithium-ion batteries and electronic waste, is recognized as essential for securing a sustainable supply for the future [4–7]. China's brine lithium resources account for 15 % of the global total and are mainly distributed in various salt lakes in Tibet, Qinghai and Xinjiang [8–10].

Presently, brine separation engineering has emerged as a pivotal approach for the extraction of freshwater and various natural elements from the extensive reserves of brine resources. In response to industrial demands and sustainable development strategies, brine separation technologies efficiently extract water and salt ions from brine resources, facilitating the rational utilization of resources [11]. This specifically involves processes of salt separation and enrichment [12]. Among the most typical applications of brine separation are seawater desalination, lithium extraction and efficient enrichment, all of which have significant market demand and economic benefits. Common brine separation technologies in industrial applications include thermal evaporation, membrane processes, and electroadsorption methods, all of which have been widely utilized [2,13,14]. However, despite their superior brine separation capabilities, these technologies have some drawbacks such as high operating temperatures and pressures, membrane fouling and scaling, as well as slow electroadsorption kinetics [15–17]. Recently, electrochemical ion separation technology has gained attention due to its environmentally friendly nature, energy efficiency, simple operation, and excellent electroadsorption kinetics [18,19].

FCDI technology, as an emerging electrochemical separation technique, demonstrates the capability for continuous regeneration of flow-electrodes, exhibiting a pseudo "infinite" electroadsorption capacity [20–22]. Therefore, FCDI holds tremendous potential in the field of brine separation such as seawater desalination and lithium extraction. In the process of FCDI, charged ions within the aqueous solution traverse the ion exchange membranes propelled by the electrostatic driving force, subsequently being adsorbed by the active electrode material in the flow-electrode, thereby completing the ions extraction process. Subsequently, in the FCDI system, the flow-electrode responsible for cation adsorption interacts externally with the flow-electrode designated for anion adsorption, leading to a process of charge neutralization within the active electrode material. During this interaction, the salt ions that are previously adsorbed onto the active electrode material are released back into the electrolyte medium. This dynamic exchange facilitates the regeneration of the electroadsorption capacity of the flow-electrode, effectively restoring its functionality for subsequent separation cycles [23–25].

In the system of FCDI, the active electrode materials serve as the essential components in the electroadsorption process, significantly contributing to the optimization of ions extract performance through their pivotal roles in ions capture and charge transfer mechanisms [26]. The innovative progress of electrode materials greatly influences the development of FCDI technology, and remarkable electrode materials are indispensable for efficient FCDI applications [27]. In recent years, considerable research efforts have been devoted to designing and developing advanced electrode materials with high specific surface area, strong chemical/physical stability, excellent conductivity, and low-cost efficient ion capture [28].

The most commonly researched and used carbon-based electrode materials currently include activated carbon, graphene, carbon nanotubes, and covalent/metal-organic framework derived carbons [29–32]. Despite their efficacy, numerous carbon-based materials are prohibitively expensive for practical applications and necessitate intricate manufacturing processes. Furthermore, during the preparation of the electrode slurry, substantial time is required for meticulous stirring to guarantee thorough impregnation and uniform dispersion of the carbon materials within the aqueous solution. Consequently, there exists a pressing and significant demand for continued innovation focused on the enhancement of material properties, the development of eco-friendly and high-performance ion-capturing electrode materials, and the attainment of superior durability and operational effectiveness.

Carbon nanodots (CNs) derived from the waste biomass, as a member of the carbon materials family, are a fascinating type of nanostructured carbon with ultra-small size and abundant surface functional groups, particularly their derivatives doped with nitrogen, boron, phosphorus or sulfur atoms, exhibit excellent stability, high specific surface area, exceptional electrochemical conductivity and outstanding dispersibility [33]. Meanwhile, doping heteroatoms (such as N, P and S) in CNs can induce pseudocapacitive activity and enhance surface wettability, thereby increasing capacitance and accelerating charge-discharge dynamics [34]. They have attracted widespread attention for promoting electrochemical applications as an affordable and sustainable alternative with properties comparable to traditional active materials, making them a promising option for large-scale engineering applications. Due to the quantum size effect and high redox activity, CNs play a crucial role in electrochemical energy storage [35]. However, there are still rarely reports on the research of CNs as active electrode materials for FCDI.

Inspired by the above, we designed and developed an advanced N-CNs flow-electrode using nitrogen-rich waste bean shells as the precursor material through a one-step hydrothermal method. The flow-electrode exhibits enhanced chemical and physical stability, excellent conductivity, and cost-effective lithium extraction capabilities. N-CNs solution can provide more electroadsorption sites, accelerate ion diffusion kinetics, and speed up electron transfer rate. Its main advantage is that it is a pure carbon dot without metal or polymer modification, thus achieving efficient electroadsorption and extraction of Li+ ions.

Bean shells were acquired from the nearby farm produce market in Zhenjiang (Jiangsu Provience, China). Deionized water (18.2 MΩ) was used in all experiments. Commercial activated carbon (AC) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Cellulose membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 was purchased from Shanghai Baoman Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The ion exchange membranes (520 mm × 1130 mm) were all purchased from Zhejiang Lanran New Materials Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CNs derived from bean shells
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped carbon nanodots solution was obtained through a one-step hydrothermal reaction. Inexpensive and N-rich bean shells were used as the biomass carbon source. After cleaning fresh bean shells, they were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h, and then crushed into powder using a pulverizer. 3 g of biomass powder was placed in a 100 mL reaction flask, 50 mL of H2O was added and stirred evenly. The reaction flask was then placed in an oven at 180 °C for 15 h, and the reaction was completed and naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was filtered with a 0.2 μm cellulose membrane, and then dialyzed for 48 h to remove other impurities from the solution, resulting in an orange-yellow solution of N-doped carbon nanodots (named N-CNs). The concentration of the N-CNs solution was approximately 3.0 mg mL−1, which suggested that the yield of the nanodots was around 5 %.

The morphology of the samples was examined using a High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM, model: JEOL 2010, operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV). The phase composition was assessed with a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, model: Philips X-Pert Pro Super), which utilized a Cu Kα X-ray source with a wavelength of λ = 1.5418 Å. Data regarding the functional groups present in the samples were acquired through Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR, model: Nexus). The elemental composition of the samples was investigated using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250), employing Al Kα radiation (E = 1486.6 eV) as the source.

All electrochemical performances were evaluated using an electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, CHI660E). Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were carried out in a three-electrode configuration, employing a 1 mol L−1 LiCl solution as the electrolyte, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum mesh counter electrode, and the sample serving as the working electrode. The GCD measurements were conducted within a voltage range of −0.2 to −0.8 V at current densities of 1 A g−1. The electrode material was synthesized by combining the active material, carbon black and PVDF in a mass ratio of 8:1:1. Anhydrous ethanol was added to the mixture, which was then sonicated for 30 min. The resulting mixture was thoroughly ground in a mortar to achieve a uniform slurry for the electrode material. This slurry was subsequently drop-cast onto nickel foam and cured in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The specific capacitance of the sample (C, F g−1) was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
C=IΔt/mΔV
where I is the discharge current density, Δt is the discharge time, m is the mass of the active material, and ΔV is the discharge voltage difference.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) experiments evaluate the charge transfer and mass transfer processes differences between N-CNs and AC flow- electrodes in relation to FCDI, thereby analyzing the resistance of FCDI electrodes under various conditions. The test employs a two-electrode system, with the testing voltage set at 0 V, a frequency range of 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz and an amplitude of 10 mV. A two-electrode mode is adopted (with the reference electrode short-circuited to the counter electrode), as illustrated in Scheme 1.

The device and operation mode of FCDI is shown in the Scheme 2 with the collector made of a graphite plate (80 mm × 80 mm × 8 mm) and a carved serpentine channel (40 mm long, 2 mm wide and 2 mm high). The effective contact area between the ion exchange membranes and the flow-electrodes is 7.228 cm2. Silicone gaskets are used between the cation exchange membrane (CEM), anion exchange membrane (AEM), water channel plate, and collector to prevent liquid leakage. An acrylic plate of the same model is used to protect the outer end of the collector. All components are fastened together with bolts to prevent leaks. To avoid short circuits, a nylon isolation pillar is placed at the screw hole of one of the collectors. The flow rate of the feed solution and flow-electrodes is controlled by a peristaltic pump, while a LANHE (CT3002A) testing system applies voltage and records current data. The variation in solution conductivity was monitored using a conductivity meter (DDSJ 307 F, Leici). To evaluate the optimal operating conditions for the N-CNs solution electrode, a systematic investigation was conducted on the effects of different applied voltages (1.0–3.0 V), different initial concentrations of LiCl solution (0.001 mol L−1, 0.005 mol L−1, 0.01 mol L−1, 0.05 mol L−1, and 0.1 mol L−1) and different test temperatures on electroadsorption capacity, electroadsorption rate, and charge efficiency.

The performance of FCDI was represented by several metrics, including gravimetric electroadsorption capacity (GAC), gravimetric electroadsorption rate (GAR), average salt removal rate (ASRR), and charge efficiency (CE) [36]. These metrics were calculated using the following formulas:
(2)
A S S R =
(
C
t
−
C
0
)
× q
A
(3)
C E =
F × V ×
(
C
0
−
C
t
)
M ×
∫ 0 t
I d t
(4)
G A C =
(
C
0
−
C
t
)
V
m
(5)
G A C =
(
C
0
− C t
)
V
m t
Where C
0 (mg L−1) and C
t (mg L−1) represent the salt concentration before electroadsorption and the final concentration after electroadsorption completion, respectively, q (mL min−1) is the flow rate within the electrode chamber, A (cm2) is the effective contact area between the ion exchange membrane and the flow-electrode, F is the Faraday constant (96485 C), M is the molar mass of LiCl (g mol−1), I (mA) is the current, m (mg) is the mass of the electrolyte in the electrode slurry, and t (min) is the electroadsorption time.

A schematic illustration for the N-CNs preparation process was shown in the Fig. 1a, inexpensive and abundantly available bean shells were employed as a raw material using a one-step hydrothermal method, followed by purification through filtration and dialysis. Fig. 1b showed the TEM image of N-CNs obtained from bean shells powder under hydrothermal conditions at 180 °C for 15 h, it can be observed that their size was uniformly distributed between 3 and 7 nm. The HR-TEM image (inserted as Fig. 1b) displayed well-resolved lattice fringes, with an interplanar distance of 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon [37]. The crystal phase of N-CNs was characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1c, N-CNs exhibited a prominent diffraction peak at 2θ = 26°, corresponding to the (002) plane of carbon materials, which is consistent with the HR-TEM results. The illustration showed a photograph of the fluorescence effect of the nanodots in the Fig. 1c. Fig. 1d presents the FTIR spectrum of N-CNs derived from bean shells. It can be noted that the peak at 3410 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H, and the peak at 2960 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N-H. The absorption peaks at 1620 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, and 1120 cm−1 correspond to the vibration absorption peaks of H-O and CO/CN and the bending absorption peak of C-O/C-N, respectively [38]. FTIR analysis revealed that the N-CNs derived from bean shells are rich in nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on their surface [39].

The XPS analysis of N-CNs indicates that the elemental composition comprises 83.0 % carbon, 14.4 % oxygen, and 2.6 % nitrogen ( Fig. 2a). The C1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2b, four peaks corresponding to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.5 eV), CO (289.5 eV), and C–N (285.1 eV) can be observed. The O1s spectrum further validates the presence of CO (531.5 eV), C–O–C (532.5 eV), and C–OH (533.7 eV) (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d reveals three distinct nitrogen functional groups, pyridinic N (397.8 eV), pyrrolic N (399.7 eV), and graphitic N (402.2 eV) [40].

In the performance evaluation of capacitors, the N-CNs electrode exhibited significant moisture absorption and highly solubility in water after drying. Consequently, we promptly conducted tests on the electrodes in a 1 mol L−1 LiCl solution immediately after preparation. Despite multiple attempts, we were only able to assess the capacitance performance at a current density of 1 A g−1, comparing the results with that of AC. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the GCD curve indicates that the capacitance of N-CNs at 85.3 F g−1 is noticeably greater than that of AC, which recorded a capacitance of 73.5 F g−1. Fig. 3b presents the EIS analysis of N-CNs and the AC flow-electrodes. The intersection of the EIS curves with the horizontal axis indicates the ohmic resistance of the flow-electrode. It can be observed that the ohmic resistance of N-CNs is 3.8 Ω, while that of AC is 6.7 Ω. The greater ohmic resistance of AC compared to N-CNs suggests that N-CNs possess outstanding conductivity relative to AC. Consequently, it can be inferred that the Li+ ions extraction performance of N-CNs in FCDI is superior to that of AC, primarily due to the enhanced conductivity of N-CNs.

To evaluate the Li+ ions extraction performance of N-CNs solution as flow-electrode slurry for FCDI, control experiments were conducted using deionized water and AC slurry as flow-electrode materials in a 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution at a voltage of 2.5 V, respectively. When the electrode slurry did not contain any electrode materials, it was classified as the electrodialysis (ED) process. Over time, due to the electric field driving ion migration, the conductivity of the LiCl solution exhibited a certain degree of change, with a Li+ ions permeation rate of 0.14 µmol cm−2 min−1. In contrast, the addition of flow-electrode materials (AC and N-CNs), significantly enhanced the Li+ ions extraction performance of FCDI cell. For the AC and N-CNs flow-electrode slurries, the conductivity of the feed LiCl solution decreased significantly, with ASRR of 0.49 and 0.62 µmol cm−2 min−1, respectively, indicating that the Li+ ions extraction capabilities of both FCDI cells continually improved, and the Li+ ions extraction capacity of the N-CNs electrode could rival or even slightly surpass that of the AC electrode ( Fig. 4).

Furthermore, by systematically controlling the hydrothermal reaction temperature and reaction time, a series of N-CNs solutions were obtained, which were subsequently subjected to FCDI performance testing for lithium extraction, as detailed in Table S1. It can be observed that the N-CNs obtained at a hydrothermal temperature of 180 °C with reaction time of 15 h exhibit optimal lithium extraction performance, indicating that both excessively high and low reaction temperatures, as well as prolonged reaction times, adversely affect the FCDI performance of the N-CNs. Generally, increasing the reaction temperature accelerates the reaction rate; however, excessively high temperatures can lead to the rapid formation of N-CNs during the hydrothermal reaction, which may result in the aggregation of the N-CNs. Conversely, low reaction temperatures can cause uneven particle size distribution, lower the concentration of N-CNs, and increase solution resistance, ultimately leading to decreased lithium extraction performance. Additionally, prolonged reaction times can also lead to particle aggregation, resulting in increased particle size and uneven distribution.

The flow-electrode is conveyed from the storage tank into the cathode and anode chambers through a pumping mechanism, whereupon the effluents generated in both chambers are subsequently collected and redirected back to the storage tank of the flow-electrode ( Fig. 5a). This process facilitates the regeneration of the flow-electrode within the confines of the storage tank, thereby enhancing its operational efficiency. Fig. 5b shows the variation of conductivity with time at different operating voltages (1.0 V, 1.5 V, 2.0 V, 2.5 V, and 3.0 V) in a 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution. After 120 min FCDI electroadsorption experiment, the lowest LiCl concentration was 4.1 g L−1 when the operating voltage was 3.0 V, followed by a lower concentration at 2.5 V, indicating that by increasing the operating voltage of the FCDI, the driving force for ions electroadsorption is significantly enhanced, thereby increasing the Li+ ions extraction rate. The GAC of Li+ ions was displayed in Fig. 5c, where it is evident that the electroadsorption capacity progressively increases over time at various operating voltages. After 120 min of FCDI operation, the capacities reached 35, 45, 55, 75, and 175 mg g−1, respectively. The Ragone curves gradually extended towards the upper right, further confirming the superior GAR and GAC Li+ ions performance of the N-CNs flow-electrode at higher operating potential (Fig. 5d).

Fig. 5e illustrates that the fluctuations in current during voltage application in the FCDI align with the conductivity, demonstrating that the FCDI current exhibits an upward trend in response to increasing operational voltage. The Li+ ions electroadsorption effects are further evaluated using ASRR and CE, as shown in Fig. 5f. At an operating voltage of 3 V, the ASRR value reached its peak at 1.42 μmol cm−2 min−1, in contrast to the values recorded at lower voltages of 1.0 V, 1.5 V, and 2.0 V, which were 0.29, 0.37, and 0.45 μmol cm−2 min−1, respectively. Notably, the highest CE of 97 % was observed at an operational voltage of 2.5 V, considerably exceeding the CE values of 30 %, 63 %, 74 %, and 85 % corresponding to 1.0 V, 1.5 V, 2.0 V, and 3.0 V, respectively (Fig. 5f). The aforementioned results indicate that while elevated operating voltages contribute to enhanced electroadsorption capacity and rates, they also precipitate a marked increase in operating current, which consequently leads to a reduction in charge efficiency.

To further investigate the performance of N-CNs flow-electrode in the FCDI system for the treatment of LiCl solutions with varying concentrations, extraction experiments for Li⁺ ions are conducted using 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, and 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solutions at a working voltage of 2.5 V, as illustrated in Fig. 6a–b. It is evident that an increase in the concentration of the feed LiCl solution correlates with an enhancement in the current, ASRR, and CE of the flow-electrode system. For the N-CNs flow-electrode, at a lower concentration of LiCl solution (0.001 mol L−1), the ASRR is 0.06 µmol cm−2 min−1 and the CE was 21 %, which may be attributed to higher solution resistance due to the lower salt concentration [41]. The ASRR values are 0.08, 0.44, 0.53, and 0.62 µmol cm−2 min−1, and the CE values are 39 %, 48 %, 75 %, and 97 % corresponding to LiCl solution concentrations of 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, and 0.1 mol L−1, respectively.

A comprehensive experiment was undertaken to investigate the effects of temperature on the lithium extraction performance of the FCDI system. To achieve precise temperature control, the flow-electrodes composed of N-CNs, along with the LiCl solution, were maintained within a range of 10–50 °C, utilizing a water bath controller. The applied voltage was set at 2.5 V, with a corresponding flow rate of 15 mL L−1. The findings revealed a notable enhancement in the lithium extraction performance of the FCDI system as the test temperature increased. Specifically, within the temperature range of 10–50 °C, the ASRR exhibited a significant rise from 0.57 µmol cm−2 min−1 to 0.81 µmol cm−2 min−1, while the CE demonstrated a slight increase from 95.0 % to 96.2 % (Fig. S1). This improvement may be attributed to the accelerated charge transfer rates at elevated temperatures, which enhance the electro-adsorption kinetics of the N-CNs. To evaluate the stability of N-CNs flow-electrode in continuous Li+ ions extraction operations, FCDI tests are conducted for over 30 h using a 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution at 2.5 V. As shown in Fig. 6c, the cycling stability of the current demonstrates a favorable performance with prolonged FCDI duration. After 30 h FCDI cycling test, the ASRR and CE remained at 69.4 % and 71.9 % of their initial values, respectively, compared to the first cycle (Fig. 6d). This decline can be attributed to the progressive accumulation of Li⁺ ions concentration within the flow-electrode slurry, which induces reverse concentration diffusion, thereby resulting in a reduction in both ASRR and CE. Notwithstanding this, the N-CNs flow-electrode exhibits considerable potential for Li⁺ ions extraction, thereby affirming the distinctive structural advantages of N-CNs that facilitate a continuous and stable FCDI process. Moreover, as illustrated in Table S2, a comparative analysis with related studies in the field of FCDI demonstrates that N-CNs exhibit superior FCDI performance when contrasted with other flow-electrode materials. This underscores the promising application prospects of N-CNs flow-electrode in the continuous treatment of high-concentration solutions.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a highly conductive N-CNs solution as a flow-electrode utilizing waste bean shells with inherent nitrogen doping through a one-step hydrothermal approach. The resulting flow-electrode exhibits commendable rheological properties, exceptional dispersibility, and enhanced hydrophilicity, which collectively facilitate the efficient transport of electrons and ions, culminating in remarkable performance in FCDI. Electrochemical assessments reveal that the N-CNs flow-electrode demonstrates superior capacitance and impedance characteristics in comparison to AC electrodes, effectively reducing charge transfer resistance and enhancing ions diffusion. The N-CNs flow-electrode showcases impressive Li⁺ ions extraction capabilities, achieving a GAC of 75 mg g−1, an ASRR of 0.62 µmol min−1 cm−2 and a CE of 97 % in 0.1 mol L−1 LiCl solution at 2.5 V. Continuous FCDI experiments show that both the ASRR and CE maintained 69.4 % and 71.9 % of their initial values, respectively, even after 30 h. The introduction of N-CNs enriches the diversity of flow-electrode materials, enabling their application as both electrode slurries and conductive additives, thereby advancing the development of FCDI technology in the realm of electron and ion transfer.

Zhou Hongjian: Writing – review & editing. Dai Weiji: Writing – review & editing. Zhang Yudong: Writing – review & editing. Cui Can: Conceptualization. Huang Saifang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Zhang Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhao Cuijiao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wang Shuyu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zhao Mengyu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Liu Menglong: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52202093), Jiangsu Specially Appointed Professorship Foundation (Grant No. 1064902003), and Jiangsu Provincial Double-Innovation Doctor Program (Grant No. JSSCBS20221297 and JSSCBS20221258).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.115677.
